name: Rhythm Timing
description: Player input is evaluated against a beat grid or scheduled cue, producing graded results based on offset.
design_notes: Rhythm timing succeeds when players can trust the audio, animation, and judgment result to describe the same event. Designers need explicit beat_grid_id data, input_offset_ms calibration, early and late hit windows, and a policy for whether gameplay simulation or audio playback is the authority.
design_purpose: Reward precise input without hiding why a hit, miss, or grade occurred.
Synchronize combat, traversal, or scoring to a musical or metronomic structure.
Give designers tunable judgment bands for accessibility, mastery, and difficulty modes.
player_fantasy: The player feels locked into the beat and sees the game respond to their timing skill.

parameters.name: beat_grid_id
parameters.description: Identifier for the cue grid or chart that maps musical beats to gameplay judgments.

parameters.name: input_offset_ms
parameters.description: Player or platform calibration offset applied before judging buffered input against the beat grid.

parameters.name: early_hit_window_ms
parameters.description: Milliseconds before the target beat that still count as an accepted early input.

parameters.name: late_hit_window_ms
parameters.description: Milliseconds after the target beat that still count as an accepted late input.

parameters.name: judgment_table_id
parameters.description: Data table that converts signed input error into score, effects, hit markers, or combo changes.

edge_cases: Audio playback drifts from the simulation clock after device sleep, tab throttling, pause, or buffer underrun.
Input buffering captures a press before a scene transition and replays it against the next chart section.
Frame drops delay visual hit markers while audio and judgment state already advanced.
Online play requires server validation but the local client must still feel immediate and musical.
common_bugs: Judgment uses frame delta instead of the audio clock, making the same input grade differently across hardware.
Calibration offset is applied twice to held notes or not applied to release events.
The hit marker displays the predicted grade but combo state uses the corrected authority result.
Beat indices wrap incorrectly in looping music and cause a perfect input to be scored against the previous loop.
balancing_notes: Wider windows increase approachability, but too much width can erase the distinction between intentional rhythm and random tapping.
Separate scoring strictness from progression requirements so players can finish content without needing expert timing.
Late windows often need to be slightly more forgiving than early windows because visual reaction naturally trails audio.
accessibility_notes: Provide audio, visual, and optional haptic beat cues, and allow players to calibrate display and input latency.
Offer reduced precision modes or wider judgment tables without changing the song or encounter structure.

implementation_notes.unity: Use AudioSettings.dspTime or a dedicated audio_clock as the authority for beat lookup, then convert buffered input timestamps into chart offsets. Keep animation and VFX driven by the same beat_grid_id, and store calibration per player profile rather than per scene.
implementation_notes.godot: Read AudioServer playback position plus measured output latency for judgment, while collecting input events with timestamp metadata. Use Resources for judgment_table_id data and keep UI hit markers separate from the resolver so prediction corrections can update cleanly.
implementation_notes.unreal: Quartz clocks or a custom audio clock can schedule beat events; gameplay should resolve input against stored cue timestamps rather than Tick delta. In networked modes, send input timestamp and beat index to the server, then reconcile local hit markers with the authoritative judgment.
implementation_notes.web: Use Web Audio currentTime for chart progression and performance.now for input capture, then reconcile both through a calibration offset. Browser tab throttling and autoplay resumes should reset chart anchors instead of letting beat indices drift silently.

example_games.title: Hi-Fi Rush
example_games.note: Uses rhythm timing to grade and empower action inputs.

example_games.title: Crypt of the NecroDancer
example_games.note: Links movement and combat actions to a beat grid.
